section.title: The Prophet's Grief and Hope
section.sectionRationale: This is the center and turning point of the whole book, the longest and most intricate acrostic, three verses to each Hebrew letter. From the depths of personal anguish under the rod of God, the sufferer reaches the great confession at the heart of Scripture: the loving devotion of the LORD never ceases, His mercies are new every morning. Around that summit the poem gathers grief, hope, repentance, and a cry for vindication.
section.thesis: In the darkest personal suffering under God's hand, the sufferer deliberately calls to mind the steadfast love and unfailing mercies of the LORD, and on that ground he counsels patient waiting, calls the people to repentance, and entrusts his cause to the God who draws near and says, Do not be afraid.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the man under the rod of wrath, 3:1 to 18; the great turn, yet I have hope, His mercies are new every morning, 3:19 to 24; reflections on the goodness and justice of God, 3:25 to 39; a call to examine our ways and return to the LORD, with renewed lament, 3:40 to 54; and the LORD who drew near, with a plea for vindication against the enemies, 3:55 to 66.

units.label: The man under the rod of wrath
units.note: I am the man who has seen affliction

verses.text: I am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of God's wrath.
He has driven me away and made me walk in darkness instead of light.
Indeed, He keeps turning His hand against me all day long.
He has worn away my flesh and skin; He has shattered my bones.
He has besieged me and surrounded me with bitterness and hardship.
He has made me dwell in darkness like those dead for ages.
He has walled me in so I cannot escape; He has weighed me down with chains.
Even when I cry out and plead for help, He shuts out my prayer.
He has barred my ways with cut stones; He has made my paths crooked.
He is a bear lying in wait, a lion hiding in ambush.
He forced me off my path and tore me to pieces; He left me without help.
He bent His bow and set me as the target for His arrow.
He pierced my kidneys with His arrows.
I am a laughingstock to all my people; they mock me in song all day long.
He has filled me with bitterness; He has intoxicated me with wormwood.
He has ground my teeth with gravel and trampled me in the dust.
My soul has been deprived of peace; I have forgotten what prosperity is.
So I say, “My strength has perished, along with my hope from the LORD.”

units.label: Yet I have hope
units.note: His mercies are new every morning

verses.text: Remember my affliction and wandering, the wormwood and the gall.
Surely my soul remembers and is humbled within me.
Yet I call this to mind, and therefore I have hope:
Because of the {{chesed|loving devotion}} of the LORD we are not consumed, for His {{rachamim|mercies}} never fail.
They are new every morning; great is Your {{emunah|faithfulness}}!
“The LORD is my {{cheleq|portion}},” says my soul, “therefore I will hope in Him.”

units.label: The LORD is good
units.note: on waiting, and the justice of God

verses.text: The LORD is good to those who wait for Him, to the soul who seeks Him.
It is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD.
It is good for a man to bear the yoke while he is still young.
Let him sit alone in silence, for the LORD has laid it upon him.
Let him bury his face in the dust, perhaps there is still hope.
Let him offer his cheek to the one who would strike him; let him be filled with reproach.
For the Lord will not cast us off forever.
Even if He causes grief, He will show compassion according to His abundant loving devotion.
For He does not willingly afflict or grieve the sons of men.
To crush underfoot all the prisoners of the land,
to deny a man justice before the Most High,
to subvert a man in his lawsuit, of these the Lord does not approve.
Who has spoken and it came to pass, unless the Lord has ordained it?
Do not both adversity and good come from the mouth of the Most High?
Why should any mortal man complain, in view of his sins?

units.label: Let us return to the LORD

verses.text: Let us examine and test our ways, and turn back to the LORD.
Let us lift up our hearts and hands to God in heaven:
“We have sinned and rebelled; You have not forgiven.”
You have covered Yourself in anger and pursued us; You have killed without pity.
You have covered Yourself with a cloud that no prayer can pass through.
You have made us scum and refuse among the nations.
All our enemies open their mouths against us.
Panic and pitfall have come upon us, devastation and destruction.
Streams of tears flow from my eyes over the destruction of the daughter of my people.
My eyes overflow unceasingly, without relief,
until the LORD looks down from heaven and sees.
My eyes bring grief to my soul because of all the daughters of my city.
Without cause my enemies hunted me like a bird.
They dropped me alive into a pit and cast stones upon me.
The waters flowed over my head, and I thought I was going to die.

units.label: You drew near when I called
units.note: the LORD heard, and a plea for justice

verses.text: I called on Your name, O LORD, out of the depths of the Pit.
You heard my plea: “Do not ignore my cry for relief.”
You drew near when I called on You; You said, “Do not be afraid.”
You defend my cause, O Lord; You redeem my life.
You have seen, O LORD, the wrong done to me; vindicate my cause!
You have seen all their malice, all their plots against me.
O LORD, You have heard their insults, all their plots against me,
the slander and murmuring of my assailants against me all day long.
When they sit and when they rise, see how they mock me in song.
You will pay them back what they deserve, O LORD, according to the work of their hands.
Put a veil of anguish over their hearts; may Your curse be upon them!
You will pursue them in anger and exterminate them from under Your heavens, O LORD.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast love, covenant loyalty. This is one of the richest words in the Old Testament, God's committed, faithful love that will not let His people go. Its range includes love, kindness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant. It is the ground of all hope in 3:22.

rachamim.gloss: Mercies, tender compassions; the plural of a word related to the womb, evoking the deep, gut level tenderness a mother feels for her child. God's compassions are not worn out by our sin; they never fail and are renewed with each new morning.

emunah.gloss: Faithfulness, firmness, reliability; the word behind our Amen. God's faithfulness is not a mood but a settled, solid dependability. Great is Your faithfulness confesses that God can be leaned on completely, even when everything else has collapsed.

cheleq.gloss: Portion, share, allotted inheritance. In Israel each tribe received a portion of land, but the Levites' portion was the LORD Himself. To say the LORD is my portion is to declare that God, not possessions or circumstances, is one's true and sufficient inheritance.

basic.context: This is the heart of the book, and the most personal chapter. A single sufferer speaks, and he does not spare us: God has become like a bear, a lion, an archer aiming at him; he is walled in, chewed by gravel, drowning in bitterness, and even his prayers seem shut out. He reaches the bottom in verse 18, my hope from the LORD has perished. And then, at the exact center of Lamentations, the whole book turns. He deliberately calls one thing to mind: the LORD's steadfast love never ceases, His mercies are new every morning, great is Your faithfulness. From that summit he counsels patient waiting, calls the people to repent and return, weeps again, and finally rests his case with the God who drew near and said, Do not be afraid.
basic.hermeneutics: The famous words of verses 22 to 23 are not a naive burst of good feeling; they come out of the deepest darkness, and they are a choice. Notice verse 21: yet I call this to mind, and therefore I have hope. Hope here is an act of remembering God's character on purpose, against the evidence of feelings. The chapter teaches how faith survives suffering: not by denying the pain, which it describes with brutal honesty, but by anchoring to who God is, His covenant love, His endless mercy, His faithfulness, and then waiting, repenting, and praying.
basic.originalLanguages: The turning point rests on a cluster of great Hebrew words. Chesed is the LORD's steadfast, covenant love that never lets go. Rachamim, His mercies, is a word tied to the womb, the deep tenderness a mother feels, and it never runs dry. Emunah is faithfulness, the solid word behind our Amen, God utterly dependable. And cheleq is one's portion or inheritance; to say the LORD is my portion is to make God Himself, not comfort or possessions, one's treasure.
basic.typology: The innocent sufferer who is aimed at like a target, dropped into a pit, has water close over his head, and calls out from the depths anticipates Christ, who was truly innocent, cast into the pit of death, and heard by the Father. God's mercies new every morning point to the new mercy that dawned in the resurrection on the first day of the week. And the LORD is my portion is fulfilled in the believer whose inheritance is Christ Himself.
basic.christ: The sufferer's cry, I called on Your name out of the depths of the Pit, and You drew near and said Do not be afraid, is answered supremely in Jesus. He, the truly innocent one, went down into the pit of death, and the Father heard Him and raised Him. The steadfast love that never ceases is poured out at the cross, where mercy and justice meet, and the new mercies every morning shine brightest in the resurrection dawn. Because the LORD is our portion in Christ, we can never be finally consumed.
basic.god: This chapter gives us one of the fullest portraits of God's heart in the Bible: He is faithful, His love steadfast, His compassions inexhaustible and daily renewed. He is sovereign, both adversity and good come from His mouth, and He is just, He does not approve of injustice and does not willingly afflict. He disciplines, but He will not cast off forever, and He draws near to the crying heart with Do not be afraid.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 42 and 62 model waiting and hoping in God from the depths. Romans 8 says nothing can separate us from God's love. The hymn Great Is Thy Faithfulness draws its title from verse 23. Hebrews 12 speaks of the LORD's discipline as a Father's love, and Jonah 2 echoes crying out from the depths.
basic.oneStory: At the very center of the Bible's darkest lament stands its brightest confession of hope: the LORD's love never ceases and His mercies are new every morning. This is the hinge on which the exile, and the whole story of God's dealing with sinners, turns from despair toward hope. It runs forward to Christ, in whom that steadfast love takes flesh, goes down into the pit for us, and rises with mercy new every morning.

context.literary: The central and most elaborate poem, a triple acrostic: sixty six verses in which each successive Hebrew letter begins three verses in a row. This heightened form places the greatest structural weight on the book's turning point. The chapter moves from an individual lament, 3:1 to 18, to a confession of hope, 3:19 to 24, to wisdom reflection, 3:25 to 39, to communal repentance and lament, 3:40 to 54, to thanksgiving and imprecation, 3:55 to 66.
context.historical: The I of this chapter speaks out of the same catastrophe, the fall of Jerusalem and the exile, but voices it as intense personal suffering; the details of the pit, the stones, and the rising waters may reflect experiences like Jeremiah's imprisonment in the cistern. The call to examine our ways and return, 3:40, addresses a people reckoning with why the disaster came.
context.canonical: The confession of 3:22 to 23 is one of the high points of Old Testament theology, gathering the covenant language of Exodus 34, the LORD abounding in steadfast love, into the middle of the exile. It grounds the hope that runs through the prophets and finds its fulfillment in the mercy of God revealed in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead God's suffering people from the depths of anguish to hope, by deliberately calling to mind the LORD's steadfast love, unfailing mercies, and faithfulness; and on that ground to counsel patient waiting, honest repentance, and confident prayer to the God who hears from the pit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter both describes one man's suffering and prescribes a pattern for all sufferers: remember God's character on purpose, 3:21 to 24; wait quietly for His salvation, 3:25 to 26; examine your ways and return to Him, 3:40; and call on His name from the depths, 3:55. The imprecation of 3:64 to 66 describes entrusting justice to God rather than commanding personal revenge.

debates.question: Who is the I, the man who has seen affliction, in 3:1?

views.label: The prophet, traditionally Jeremiah, or an individual sufferer
views.case: The vivid personal details, the pit, the stones, the mocking song, fit a specific suffering man, and tradition connects the book with Jeremiah; the chapter reads as one person's testimony.

views.label: A representative figure voicing the whole community
views.case: The lament slides into we and us, 3:22, 3:40 to 47, so the individual speaks on behalf of Zion; his experience gives voice to the corporate suffering of God's people in exile.

debates.bearing: Either way the individual becomes the model sufferer whose movement from despair to hope teaches the whole community, and the church, how to grieve and trust; Christian reading also hears the voice of Christ, the true sufferer, in these words.

debates.question: How do the tender mercies of 3:22 to 33 fit with the imprecation of 3:64 to 66?

views.label: A tension to be held, not resolved away
views.case: The same chapter that celebrates God's compassion also asks Him to repay the enemies; both belong to honest faith, which longs for mercy and also for justice against real evil.

views.label: Justice entrusted to God, not seized by self
views.case: The sufferer does not take revenge; he hands the enemies' malice over to the righteous Judge, 3:64, which is exactly what the New Testament commends, leaving room for the wrath of God.

debates.bearing: The prayer for justice is not the opposite of trusting God's mercy but part of it: because God is faithful and just, the sufferer can release vengeance to Him and rest, as Christ did, in the One who judges justly.

study.originalLanguages: The hinge of the book turns on four great words. Chesed, in 3:22, is the LORD's steadfast, covenant love, His committed loyalty that will not let His people go; it is why we are not consumed. Rachamim, His mercies, is bound up with the Hebrew for womb, the visceral tenderness of a mother, and these compassions never fail but are renewed every morning. Emunah, faithfulness, is the firm, dependable word behind our Amen: great is Your faithfulness confesses a God who can bear all our weight. And cheleq, portion, is the language of inheritance; as the Levites' portion was the LORD Himself, so the sufferer stakes everything on God as his treasure, therefore I will hope in Him.

typology.type: The innocent sufferer set as a target and cast into the pit, 3:12, 3:53 to 55
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the truly innocent one, delivered to death and the grave, who cried from the depths and was heard and raised.
The mercy that dawned in the resurrection on the first morning of the week, forever new in Christ.
The believer whose inheritance is God Himself in Christ, an inheritance that can never perish, spoil, or fade.

study.christ: The deepest voice in this chapter is finally the voice of Christ. He is the man who truly saw affliction, set as the target of wrath, mocked in song, cast into the pit of death, with the waters of judgment closing over His head. He called on the Father's name from the depths, and the Father drew near, raising Him and saying in effect, Do not be afraid. At the cross the steadfast love that never ceases and the justice that repays evil meet and are both satisfied. And because Jesus is risen, the mercies of God are new every morning for all who are His, and the LORD Himself is our portion forever.
study.god: Here God is unveiled as gloriously good: His love is steadfast, His compassions are inexhaustible and freshly given each dawn, and His faithfulness is great. He is sovereign over both adversity and good, yet He is not the author of injustice and does not afflict from His heart, that is, willingly or gladly. He disciplines His people but refuses to cast them off forever, and He stoops to the crying voice in the pit with the tenderest word in the book, Do not be afraid. This is the God on whom hope can safely rest.

crossReferences.note: The LORD proclaims Himself abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness, the covenant language behind Lamentations 3:22 to 23.
Waiting in silence for God alone, echoing the counsel to wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD.
Nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ, the fullness of we are not consumed.
The LORD disciplines those He loves as a Father, illuminating He does not willingly afflict.
Christ did not retaliate but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, the pattern behind entrusting the enemies to God.

study.oneStory: At the exact center of the Bible's longest lament shines its greatest hope: the LORD's love never ceases, His mercies are new every morning, great is His faithfulness. This confession, wrung from the depths, is the hinge on which grief turns toward hope for exiled Israel and for every sufferer since. It reaches forward to Christ, in whom steadfast love takes flesh, descends into the pit for us, and rises with mercy new every morning, so that God Himself becomes our portion forever.

sermon.title: God's Faithfulness
sermon.why: Listed on Truth For Life's own collection of Alistair Begg's Lamentations sermons as a message on Lamentations 3, this exposition centers on the steadfast love and faithfulness of God at the heart of the chapter, handled in a faithful, gospel centered way.

quiz.q: What makes Lamentations 3 the turning point of the whole book?

options.text: From the deepest personal darkness it rises to the central confession that the LORD's love never ceases and His mercies are new every morning.
options.note: The book's structural and theological center is the hope of 3:22 to 23.

options.text: It moves from despair to hope in the middle of the chapter.
options.note: True, and specifically the hope is grounded in God's steadfast love and faithfulness.

options.text: It finally announces that Jerusalem was never really destroyed.
options.note: The suffering is real; the turn is toward hope in God, not a denial of the ruin.

options.text: It records God promising never to discipline His people again.
options.note: It says He will not cast off forever, not that He will never discipline.

quiz.q: How does the sufferer arrive at hope in verses 21 to 24?

options.text: He deliberately calls God's steadfast love, mercy, and faithfulness to mind, choosing to hope on that ground.
options.note: Yet I call this to mind, and therefore I have hope: hope is an act of remembering God's character.

options.text: He decides to focus on God rather than on his pain.
options.note: True, and specifically he anchors to God's covenant love, endless mercy, and faithfulness.

options.text: His circumstances suddenly improved and lifted his mood.
options.note: Nothing in his circumstances changes; the hope comes from remembering who God is.

options.text: He concludes that his suffering was meaningless.
options.note: He finds meaning and hope in God's character, not meaninglessness.

quiz.q: What does it mean to say the LORD is my portion, in verse 24?

options.text: That God Himself, not possessions or circumstances, is one's true and sufficient inheritance and treasure.
options.note: Cheleq is inheritance language; as the Levites' portion was the LORD, so is the sufferer's.

options.text: That God is the most important thing the sufferer has.
options.note: Close, and the specific image is that God is his allotted inheritance, his very portion.

options.text: That God will give him back all his lost property.
options.note: The point is God Himself as the portion, not the return of possessions.

options.text: That he owns a share of God's power.
options.note: It means God is his inheritance, not that he possesses a portion of God's power.

quiz.q: How does the chapter counsel the sufferer to respond to God's discipline?

options.text: To wait quietly for the LORD's salvation, examine and turn from sin, and call on His name from the depths.
options.note: Waiting, repentance, and prayer are the prescribed responses, 3:25 to 26, 3:40, 3:55.

options.text: To be patient and keep trusting God through the suffering.
options.note: True, and more specifically to wait, repent, and cry out to God.

options.text: To demand that God immediately explain everything.
options.note: It counsels quiet waiting and repentance rather than demands.

options.text: To abandon prayer since God had shut it out.
options.note: Though 3:8 felt prayer was shut out, the sufferer keeps calling and God draws near, 3:55 to 57.

quiz.q: How does the sufferer's cry from the pit point to Christ?

options.text: The innocent one cast into the pit, with waters over his head, who calls out and is heard, foreshadows Christ, who went into death and was raised by the Father.
options.note: Christ is the true sufferer heard from the depths, met with Do not be afraid.

options.text: It shows a suffering man rescued by God after crying out.
options.note: True, and the pattern is fulfilled supremely in the death and resurrection of Christ.

options.text: It teaches that God never lets the righteous suffer at all.
options.note: The righteous do suffer here; the hope is that God hears and delivers, ultimately in Christ.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ.
options.note: The church has long heard the voice of the suffering Christ in this chapter.

quiz.q: How do the tender mercies of God and the prayer against the enemies fit together?

options.text: The sufferer does not take revenge but entrusts justice to the righteous Judge, which flows from, rather than contradicts, trusting God's mercy.
options.note: He hands the enemies' malice to God, the pattern Christ Himself followed.

options.text: He leaves the enemies to God rather than repaying them himself.
options.note: True, and this entrusting of justice to God is itself part of resting in His faithfulness.

options.text: They simply contradict each other and cannot both be true.
options.note: Scripture holds them together: longing for mercy and for justice against real evil.

options.text: It shows the sufferer taking personal vengeance.
options.note: He asks God to judge; he does not avenge himself.

questions: The sufferer describes his pain with brutal honesty before he ever speaks of hope. Why might it matter that the Bible lets us say how bad it really is before pointing us to God's mercy?
Verse 21 says, yet I call this to mind, and therefore I have hope. What truths about God's character do you most need to deliberately call to mind when your feelings are telling you all is lost?
His mercies are new every morning. How would it change your ordinary mornings to receive each new day as a fresh delivery of God's compassion rather than just another round of demands?
To say the LORD is my portion is to treasure God more than what He gives. Honestly, is God your portion, or are you leaning your hope on something He might take away?
The sufferer entrusted the wrong done to him to God the Judge rather than avenging himself. Where do you need to hand a grievance over to God instead of carrying or repaying it yourself?

translationNotes.note: Loving devotion translates chesed, God's steadfast covenant love; the range of meaning includes love, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant.
Great is Your faithfulness, emunah, gave the title to the beloved hymn Great Is Thy Faithfulness; the word denotes firm, dependable reliability.
The BSB source prints an em dash after in the dust; it is rendered here as a clean break with a comma, with no change to the wording. The same applies to 3:36, 3:47, and 3:61.
He does not willingly afflict is literally He does not afflict from His heart; God's discipline is real but not something He delights to do.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Lamentations 3:1-66. Verse segments concatenated in order and separated by single spaces; poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Em dashes in the source at 3:29, 3:36, 3:47, and 3:61 were each converted to a clean break with a comma. No em or en dashes used anywhere in the file.
